title: US and Iran Ship Seizures Violate International Law, Shipping Body Warns
summary:
A prominent shipping organization has condemned both the United States and Iran for capturing commercial ships, calling the actions a violation of international law. The International Chamber of Shipping (ICS), which represents about 80% of the world's merchant fleet, is demanding the immediate release of all crews.

In recent days, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps seized two vessels: the Panamanian-flagged MSC Francesca and the Greek-owned Epaminondas, accusing them of operating without permits and tampering with navigation systems. The U.S. Defense Department, meanwhile, captured the Iran-linked Majestic X in the Indian Ocean for transporting sanctioned oil. Fifteen Filipino seafarers are among those held, along with crew from Montenegro and other nations.

John Stawpert, marine director of the ICS, told Al Jazeera that the seizures are an affront to freedom of navigation — a principle enshrined in international law that allows ships to pass through waters freely. "All these people are doing is transporting trade," he said. "We can't have a situation where ships are being seized for political ends."

Stawpert also criticized Iran's threat to charge tolls in the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial waterway that carries about one-fifth of the world's oil and natural gas. He warned that if Iran can do it, other countries might follow suit in straits like Gibraltar or Malacca. The U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports has added more uncertainty, he said.

The crisis has left an estimated 20,000 seafarers stranded in the Gulf for seven weeks. "The psychological burden will be beginning to tell on them," Stawpert said, calling the situation a form of house arrest. The blockage has already driven up fuel prices worldwide, forcing governments to implement emergency energy-saving measures.

Stawpert urged both sides to respect international law: "Let's resume freedom of navigation and respect the right to innocent passage as soon as we possibly can."
why_it_matters: This crisis shows how geopolitical disputes between countries can disrupt global trade, put innocent workers at risk, and drive up energy costs — affecting economies and families far from the conflict zone.

keywords.explanation: Acts of taking control of something, like a ship, by force.
Rules that most countries agree to follow in their dealings with each other.
The right of ships to travel through international waters without interference.
The collection of commercial ships that carry goods around the world.
A narrow waterway between Iran and Oman through which about 20% of the world's oil passes.

questions.question: Which organization condemned the ship seizures by the US and Iran?
What did Iran accuse the seized ships of doing?
options: Operating without permits and tampering with navigation systems
questions.correct_answer: Operating without permits and tampering with navigation systems

What did the International Chamber of Shipping demand?
What threat did Iran make regarding the Strait of Hormuz?
options: Charge tolls for ships passing through
questions.correct_answer: Charge tolls for ships passing through

questions.question: How many seafarers are estimated to be stranded in the Gulf due to the crisis?

background_read: The International Chamber of Shipping (ICS) is a global trade organization that represents about 80% of the world’s merchant fleet — ships that carry cargo and trade across oceans. It often speaks for ship owners on legal and safety issues.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) is a branch of Iran’s military that operates separately from the regular army and has its own navy. It has been involved in seizing ships in the Persian Gulf as part of Iran’s tensions with the United States.

The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway between Iran and the Arabian Peninsula. About one-fifth of the world’s oil and natural gas passes through it, making it a critical choke point for global energy supplies.

The principle of freedom of navigation is a key part of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), an international treaty that most countries follow. It says ships of all nations can pass through international waters and straits without being stopped, as long as they don't threaten the coastal country. Both the US and Iran have signed parts of UNCLOS, but their actions are now in dispute.
Article_Structure: LEAD: International Chamber of Shipping condemns US and Iran for seizing commercial ships, calling it a violation of international law.
└─ specific: 'demanding immediate release of all crews'
├─ IRAN: Seized MSC Francesca and Epaminondas; accused them of operating without permits and tampering with navigation systems.
└─ US: Seized Iran-linked Majestic X in Indian Ocean for transporting sanctioned oil.
PEOPLE AFFECTED: 15 Filipino seafarers and crew from Montenegro among those held.
├─ ICS STANCE: 'All these people are doing is transporting trade' (John Stawpert).
└─ GOVERNMENT ACTIONS: Both sides claim legal justification, but ICS says it's political.
├─ ENERGY: Strait of Hormuz threat could raise fuel prices worldwide.
OPEN QUESTION: Can international law be enforced when major powers ignore it?

perspectives.description: The United States argues that seizing the Iran-linked ship carrying sanctioned oil is a legitimate enforcement of international sanctions. From this view, it stops Iran from funding activities that threaten global security.

Regardless of legal arguments, seafarers are caught in the middle. They are not combatants, yet they face detention, uncertainty, and psychological stress. The ICS argues that using trade ships as pawns in political games is unacceptable.

International law on freedom of navigation is clear in principle, but enforcement is weak. Both the US and Iran have acted in ways that stretch legal interpretations. This case highlights the difficulty of applying law when powerful nations disagree.

The crisis may push countries to strengthen international agreements on maritime rights and seafarer protections. It could also lead to new rules preventing the use of commercial ships as tools of political leverage.
